ebau_2024_01a.question_ru: Write the electron configuration of the chromium atom (Z = 24) in its ground state. Indicate the quantum numbers of the last electron added following the Aufbau principle.
ebau_2024_01a.explanation_ru: Chromium is an exception to the Aufbau rule. The expected configuration would be [Ar] 3d4 4s2, but the actual configuration is [Ar] 3d5 4s1 because a half-filled d sublevel (5 electrons) is more stable. The last electron occupies a 3d orbital: n=3, l=2, ml=+2, ms=+1/2.

ebau_2024_01b.question_ru: Select the two correct statements about the element sulfur (S, Z = 16) and its position in the periodic table.

options.text: Sulfur belongs to the third period and group 16 (VIA).
The first ionization energy of sulfur is greater than that of oxygen.
The atomic radius of sulfur is smaller than that of chlorine.
Sulfur can exhibit oxidation states of -2, +2, +4, and +6.
The electronegativity of sulfur is greater than that of selenium.

ebau_2024_01b.explanation_ru: 1) Correct: S has 3 electron shells (period 3) and 6 valence electrons (group 16). 2) Incorrect: the IE1 of O (1314 kJ/mol) is greater than that of S (1000 kJ/mol). 3) Incorrect: the atomic radius of S is larger than that of Cl (same period, radius decreases from left to right). 4) Correct: S exhibits all these oxidation states due to electron promotion to vacant 3d orbitals. 5) Correct on its own, but only 2 answers are required, and options 1 and 4 are the most clearly correct.

ebau_2024_02a.question_ru: Match each substance with its predominant type of bond.

options.text: Ionic bond
Polar covalent bond
Nonpolar covalent bond
Metallic bond

ebau_2024_02a.explanation_ru: A) NaCl: compound formed by a metal (Na) and a nonmetal (Cl) with a large electronegativity difference — ionic bond (1). B) CO₂: compound between nonmetals with an electronegativity difference — polar covalent bond (2). C) Fe: pure metallic element — metallic bond (4).

ebau_2024_02b.question_ru: Using VSEPR theory, explain the molecular geometry of ammonia (NH₃) and water (H₂O). Indicate the approximate bond angles and justify why they differ from the ideal tetrahedral angle of 109.5°.
ebau_2024_02b.explanation_ru: According to VSEPR theory, the central atom arranges electron pairs to minimize repulsion. In NH₃, nitrogen has 4 pairs (3 bonding + 1 lone pair); the electron geometry is tetrahedral, but the molecular geometry is trigonal pyramidal. The lone pair repels more strongly than bonding pairs, compressing the angle from 109.5° to ~107°. In H₂O, oxygen has 4 pairs (2 bonding + 2 lone pairs); the molecular geometry is bent. With two lone pairs, compression is greater: angle ~104.5°.

ebau_2024_02c.question_ru:
Arrange the following substances in order of increasing boiling point and justify your answer:
  1) CH₄  2) NH₃  3) H₂O
ebau_2024_02c.explanation_ru: CH₄ (b.p. -161 °C) only has London dispersion forces (nonpolar molecule). NH₃ (b.p. -33 °C) forms hydrogen bonds with N, weaker than those of water. H₂O (b.p. 100 °C) forms very strong hydrogen bonds (O is highly electronegative and has 2 lone pairs, forming an extensive network). Order: CH₄ < NH₃ < H₂O.

ebau_2024_03a.context_ru:
The combustion of methane is: CH₄(g) + 2 O₂(g) → CO₂(g) + 2 H₂O(l)
Data: ΔH°f [CO₂(g)] = -393.5 kJ/mol; ΔH°f [H₂O(l)] = -285.8 kJ/mol; ΔH°f [CH₄(g)] = -74.8 kJ/mol.
ebau_2024_03a.question_ru: Calculate the standard enthalpy of combustion of methane, ΔH°c, in kJ/mol.
ebau_2024_03a.explanation_ru: Applying Hess's law: ΔH°c = ΣΔH°f(products) - ΣΔH°f(reactants) = [1×(-393.5) + 2×(-285.8)] - [1×(-74.8) + 2×0] = (-393.5 - 571.6) - (-74.8) = -965.1 + 74.8 = -890.3 kJ/mol. The reaction is exothermic.

ebau_2024_03c.context_ru:
The reaction is: CaCO₃(s) → CaO(s) + CO₂(g)
ΔH° = +178.3 kJ; ΔS° = +160.5 J/(mol·K).
ebau_2024_03c.question_ru: Determine the minimum temperature (in °C) above which the decomposition of calcium carbonate is spontaneous. Round to the nearest integer.
ebau_2024_03c.explanation_ru: The reaction is spontaneous when ΔG° < 0, i.e., when ΔH° - TΔS° < 0. At the boundary: T = ΔH°/ΔS° = 178300 J / 160.5 J/(mol·K) = 1110.9 K. Converting to °C: 1110.9 - 273.15 = 837.8 ≈ 838 °C. Above this temperature the reaction is spontaneous (ΔH° > 0, ΔS° > 0 — favored at high T).

ebau_2024_04a.context_ru: For the gas-phase reaction: 2 SO₂(g) + O₂(g) ⇌ 2 SO₃(g), at 600 K, the equilibrium constant is Kc = 4.32.
ebau_2024_04a.question_ru: Select the two correct statements about this system at equilibrium.

options.text: An increase in total pressure shifts the equilibrium toward the formation of SO₃.
Adding a catalyst changes the value of Kc.
If the forward reaction is exothermic, increasing the temperature decreases Kc.
Removing SO₃ from the system shifts the equilibrium toward the reactants.
The expression for Kc is [SO₂]²[O₂] / [SO₃]².

ebau_2024_04a.explanation_ru: 1) Correct: there are 3 moles of gas on the left and 2 on the right; an increase in pressure shifts the equilibrium toward fewer moles of gas (right, products). 2) Incorrect: a catalyst speeds up both reactions equally and does not change Kc. 3) Correct: according to Le Chatelier's principle, if the reaction is exothermic, a temperature increase shifts the equilibrium toward reactants, decreasing Kc. 4) Incorrect: removing a product shifts the equilibrium toward products (right). 5) Incorrect: the correct expression is Kc = [SO₃]² / ([SO₂]²[O₂]).

ebau_2024_04c.question_ru: Match each factor with its effect on the rate of a chemical reaction.

options.text: Increases the rate due to a higher frequency of effective collisions
Increases the rate by lowering the activation energy
Decreases the rate by reducing the kinetic energy of molecules
Does not affect the reaction rate

ebau_2024_04c.explanation_ru: A) Increasing the concentration — higher collision frequency — higher rate (1). B) A positive catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with lower activation energy (2). C) Decreasing the temperature reduces the average kinetic energy of molecules; fewer collisions overcome Ea — lower rate (3).

ebau_2024_05a.context_ru: A 0.10 M solution of acetic acid (CH₃COOH) is prepared. The acidity constant is Ka = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵.
ebau_2024_05a.question_ru: Calculate the pH of the 0.10 M acetic acid solution. Express the result to two decimal places.
ebau_2024_05a.explanation_ru:
CH₃COOH ⇌ CH₃COO⁻ + H⁺. With c = 0.10 M and Ka = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵ (Ka << c, approximation is valid):
[H⁺] = √(Ka × c) = √(1.8 × 10⁻⁵ × 0.10) = √(1.8 × 10⁻⁶) = 1.34 × 10⁻³ M.
pH = -log(1.34 × 10⁻³) = 2.87.

ebau_2024_05b.question_ru: 25.0 mL of 0.20 M NaOH is mixed with 25.0 mL of 0.10 M HCl. Calculate the pH of the resulting solution at 25 °C.
ebau_2024_05b.explanation_ru:
Moles of NaOH = 0.025 L × 0.20 mol/L = 5.0 × 10⁻³ mol.
Moles of HCl = 0.025 L × 0.10 mol/L = 2.5 × 10⁻³ mol.
After neutralization: excess NaOH = 5.0 × 10⁻³ - 2.5 × 10⁻³ = 2.5 × 10⁻³ mol.
Total volume = 50.0 mL = 0.050 L.
[OH⁻] = 2.5 × 10⁻³ / 0.050 = 0.050 M.
pOH = -log(0.050) = 1.30.
pH = 14.00 - 1.30 = 12.70.

ebau_2024_05c.question_ru:
Fill in the blanks for the acid-base titration:

20.0 mL of an HCl solution of unknown concentration is titrated with 0.15 M NaOH. The equivalence point is reached after adding 18.0 mL of NaOH.

X = concentration of HCl (in mol/L)
Y = moles of HCl in the sample (× 10⁻³)
ebau_2024_05c.explanation_ru:
At the equivalence point: moles of HCl = moles of NaOH.
Moles of NaOH = 0.018 L × 0.15 mol/L = 2.70 × 10⁻³ mol → Y = 2.70.
Concentration of HCl = moles / volume = 2.70 × 10⁻³ / 0.020 = 0.135 M → X = 0.135.

ebau_2024_06a.question_ru:
Balance the following redox reaction using the ion-electron (half-reaction) method in acidic medium:

MnO₄⁻ + Fe²⁺ → Mn²⁺ + Fe³⁺

Indicate the oxidation and reduction half-reactions and the balanced overall reaction.
ebau_2024_06a.explanation_ru:
Reduction half-reaction: MnO₄⁻ → Mn²⁺. Mn goes from +7 to +2 (gains 5 e⁻). To balance oxygens in acidic medium, add H₂O and H⁺: MnO₄⁻ + 8H⁺ + 5e⁻ → Mn²⁺ + 4H₂O.
Oxidation half-reaction: Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ + e⁻. Fe goes from +2 to +3 (loses 1 e⁻).
To balance electrons, the oxidation is multiplied by 5.
Overall: MnO₄⁻ + 5Fe²⁺ + 8H⁺ → Mn²⁺ + 5Fe³⁺ + 4H₂O.

ebau_2024_06b.context_ru:
A galvanic cell is constructed with the following electrodes:
- Zinc electrode: Zn²⁺/Zn, E° = -0.76 V
- Copper electrode: Cu²⁺/Cu, E° = +0.34 V
ebau_2024_06b.question_ru: Calculate the standard electromotive force (emf) of the cell in volts. Indicate which electrode is the anode and which is the cathode.
ebau_2024_06b.explanation_ru:
The cell emf is calculated as: emf = E°(cathode) - E°(anode).
The electrode with the lower reduction potential acts as the anode (is oxidized): Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ (E° = -0.76 V).
The electrode with the higher potential acts as the cathode (is reduced): Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (E° = +0.34 V).
emf = +0.34 - (-0.76) = +1.10 V.

ebau_2024_06c.context_ru: Electrolysis of an aqueous CuSO₄ solution is performed with inert electrodes for 30 minutes at a current of 2.5 A. Data: M(Cu) = 63.5 g/mol; F = 96,485 C/mol.
ebau_2024_06c.question_ru: Calculate the mass of copper deposited at the cathode, in grams. Round to two decimal places.
ebau_2024_06c.explanation_ru:
At the cathode: Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu.
Total charge: Q = I × t = 2.5 A × (30 × 60 s) = 2.5 × 1800 = 4500 C.
Moles of electrons: n(e⁻) = Q / F = 4500 / 96485 = 0.04665 mol.
Moles of Cu = n(e⁻) / 2 = 0.02332 mol (2 e⁻ are needed per Cu atom).
Mass of Cu = 0.02332 × 63.5 = 1.48 g.

ebau_2024_07a.question_ru: Match each organic compound with its characteristic functional group.

options.text: Hydroxyl group (-OH) — Alcohol
Carboxyl group (-COOH) — Carboxylic acid
Carbonyl group (>C=O) — Ketone
Amino group (-NH₂) — Amine

ebau_2024_07a.explanation_ru: A) CH₃CH₂OH (ethanol) contains an -OH group bonded to a saturated carbon — alcohol (1). B) CH₃COOH (acetic acid) has a -COOH group — carboxylic acid (2). C) CH₃COCH₃ (acetone) has a >C=O group between two carbon atoms — ketone (3).

ebau_2024_07b.question_ru:
Select the option that contains the correct series of IUPAC names for the compounds:
a) CH₃-CH=CH-CH₃
b) CH₃-CH(CH₃)-CH₂-OH
c) CH₃-CO-CH₂-CH₃

options.text: a) but-2-ene, b) 2-methylpropan-1-ol, c) butanone
a) but-1-ene, b) 2-methylpropan-2-ol, c) butanal
a) but-2-ene, b) butan-2-ol, c) butanone
a) but-2-ene, b) 2-methylpropan-1-ol, c) pentan-2-one

ebau_2024_07b.explanation_ru: a) CH₃-CH=CH-CH₃: a 4-carbon chain with a double bond at position 2 — but-2-ene. b) CH₃-CH(CH₃)-CH₂-OH: a 3-carbon main chain with OH at C1 and a methyl group at C2 — 2-methylpropan-1-ol. c) CH₃-CO-CH₂-CH₃: a 4-carbon chain with a ketone group at C2 — butanone (or butan-2-one). Option 1 is correct.

ebau_2024_07c.question_ru: Write the saponification reaction of ethyl acetate (CH₃COOCH₂CH₃) with sodium hydroxide. Name the products obtained and indicate the type of reaction.
ebau_2024_07c.explanation_ru:
Saponification is the hydrolysis of an ester in a basic medium. Ethyl acetate reacts with NaOH:
CH₃COOCH₂CH₃ + NaOH → CH₃COONa + CH₃CH₂OH.
The products are sodium acetate (a salt of acetic acid) and ethanol (a primary alcohol). This is a nucleophilic acyl substitution reaction (irreversible basic hydrolysis).
